For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 11 of about 48 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 10 actions returned the board to a state already seen 10 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 10 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is NOT over anymore! The click worked - greens went from 14 to 13. The "game over" state was temporary or was reset. Now I can continue playing.
I need to collect all 13 remaining green blocks, then find the pink cross and navigate to it.
Let me collect all remaining green blocks efficiently. I should batch the clicks.
Looking at the image, the green blocks are in a 2x7 grid (now with one missing from the left). The remaining blocks are at various positions.
Let me collect all remaining greens in one batch.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Game resumed! Click worked - 13 greens remaining. Need to collect all greens then find pink cross.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green blocks: {len(greens)}")

# Collect all remaining green blocks in one batch
clicks = []
for g in greens:
    boundary = g['boundary']
    center_row = sum(p[0] for p in boundary) // len(boundary)
    center_col = sum(p[1] for p in boundary) // len(boundary)
    clicks.append({'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': center_row, 'col': center_col})

result = action(clicks)
print(f"Collected {len(greens)}. Level completed: {result.get('level_completed')}, Game over: {result.get('game_over')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
pink = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'P']
player = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b'][0]
boundary = player['boundary']
player_col = sum(p[1] for p in boundary) // len(boundary)
player_row = sum(p[0] for p in boundary) // len(boundary)
print(f"Remaining greens: {len(greens)}, Pink: {len(pink)}")
print(f"Player at ({player_row}, {player_col})")

# If pink cross appeared, navigate to it
if pink:
    p = pink[0]
    p_boundary = p['boundary']
    pink_col = sum(pt[1] for pt in p_boundary) // len(p_boundary)
    pink_row = sum(pt[0] for pt in p_boundary) // len(p_boundary)
    print(f"Pink cross at ({pink_row}, {pink_col})")
    
    # Navigate to pink cross
    if pink_col > player_col:
        direction = 'RIGHT'
    else:
        direction = 'LEFT'
    
    # Estimate steps needed
    steps = abs(pink_col - player_col) // 6 + 2
    print(f"Moving {steps} {direction}")
    
    moves = [direction] * steps
    result = action(moves)
    print(f"Level completed: {result.get('level_completed')}")
    
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    pink = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'P']
    print(f"Pink remaining: {len(pink)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
Green blocks: 13
Collected 13. Level completed: False, Game over: True
